data.title: Corporacion America Airports: Staying Bullish After Strong Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Argentine airport operator Corporation America Airports (NYSE:CAAP) released its latest quarterly earnings earlier this week. I've been a long-time shareholder of this company and have been enjoying its ascent over the years:

With CAAP stock at fresh all-time highs, a new concession being arranged, and the latest earnings to review, it's a great time to update my outlook on the company.

CAAP Stock Lifts Off: Here's Why

The last few times I've written about Corporacion America Airports, the shares have been trading around $20. After being stuck around that level all year, however, CAAP stock has now jumped to $24. There are three main factors which have driven the price action since my prior update.

The first of these is the broader macroeconomic backdrop. Corporacion America Airports derives slightly over half its revenues and EBITDA from Argentina.

It's no secret that Argentina is a highly volatile economy. It just recently came out of a hyperinflationary period where the annual inflation rate topped 200%. While Corporacion America Airports gets paid in U.S. Dollars for international passengers arriving in Argentina, the fact that it relies on that economy for the majority of its business has been enough to scare investors historically.

As a result, CAAP shares have generally traded at much lower multiples than other comparable airport operators. The publicly-traded Mexican airports, for example, generally trade at significantly higher EV/EBITDA multiples than Corporacion America Airports even though Mexico has political and economic instability of its own.

In any case, Argentina's politics have swung to the right since 2023. Libertarian pro-business president Javier Milei created a great deal of excitement among investors, and Argentine stocks had been some of the strongest performers of the past few years. Notably, Milei also was the chief economist at Corporacion America in private life prior to becoming president. Not surprisingly, as president, he's helped push deregulation and increased competition within the Argentine airline industry, which has helped power its above-average traffic growth in recent years.

I mention this since Argentina had its midterm legislative elections in October. Analysts had been worried that Milei's party could suffer a setback in those elections. The Argentine Peso had come under pressure earlier this year, and this led to concerns about the sustainability of Milei's reform agenda. Back in September, however, I urged CAAP shareholders to avoid getting shaken out by political uncertainty.

That turned out well. In October, Argentina received U.S. financial backing ahead of that election which calmed down the situation. Milei's party went on to deliver results way ahead of expectations and Argentine stocks skyrocketed on the news. CAAP stock, for its part, jumped more than 15% in the days following the election. While Corporacion America Airports is far from the most highly-correlated bet on Argentina's macroeconomic situation, a rising tide lifts all boats; CAAP stock should benefit as investor sentiment improves.

While CAAP stock jumped on broader Argentine developments, the company's core operations continue firing on all cylinders as well.

Q3 Earnings Impress

We can see that with the company's Q3 results.

Revenues of $527 million rose 14% year-over-year. EPS of 34 cents surged from the nine cents reported in the same period of 2024. Adjusted EBITDA soared 35% higher versus the same quarter in 2024. And the company's EBITDA margin (excluding construction services) leapt more than 500 bps higher to 41.2% year-over-year.

Passenger traffic was up 9% in Q3, led by the strength in Argentina where CAAP once again posted double-digits year-over-year growth. And CAAP was able to transform that 9% rise in traffic growth into a 15% pop in aeronautical revenues and a 13% increase in non-aeronautical revenues.

If you dive into the details, you'll find a business that is benefiting from favorable traffic patterns at several of its key airports. It is also spending on big investments (such as the new shopping mall at the Brasilia airport) which can drive much higher revenues per passenger going forward.

The one bit of caution out of the Q3 report would be this comment from the CEO, Martín Eurnekian:

"Looking ahead, we expect positive traffic momentum to continue, although domestic growth in Argentina is anticipated to moderate from the exceptionally strong levels seen earlier in the year. Overall, we anticipate solid results in the fourth quarter, although not benefiting from the easier comparisons that supported third-quarter robust performance in Argentina."

That more restrained outlook is duly noted. Keep in mind, however, that other airport operators have seen their traffic level off to essentially flat results year-over-year amid a weaker global economy. It would have been a challenge for Argentina to keep up the double-digit traffic growth rate indefinitely given the global macro situation. Regardless, it appears Argentina can keep outperforming the overall global aviation market.

Making Sense of CAAP's Newest Airport

The final important item is the company's new concession. Specifically, Corporacion America Airports has reached an agreement to operate Iraq's flagship Baghdad international airport.

I will be honest, I was not especially enthused when I saw that Corporacion America Airport's next property would be Baghdad. Iraq has had various geopolitical and economic struggles over the years. Indeed, U.S. soldiers were killed in a 2003 fight to seize control of Baghdad's airport. Investors probably don't associate Iraq with a bustling commercial aviation market.

However, let me make the case for why this might be a reasonable venture. Corporacion America Airports' management team has a strategy of not just operating mature airport assets but also taking a build-and-expand approach. The company looks for opportunities where it can find underutilized or inadequate infrastructure and make large investments such as in new terminals, runways, shopping centers, etc. to generate much more tourist traffic and improve revenues per passenger that uses a CAAP facility.

The company offers potential counterparties a large investment in a concessions' airport infrastructure, thus potentially greatly boosting traffic potential at that property and creating new jobs and tax revenues for the local area. In return, CAAP is able to lock-in high guaranteed IRRs on these expenditures. If a contract is not living up to the stipulated IRR, it gets some sort of compensation; for example, see the Argentine contracts, which the government extended from 2028 to 2038 as relief during the pandemic lockdowns.

I haven't seen the exact details of the Iraq contract yet, but based on past experience, it seems likely that CAAP will be able to protect its funds as it expands and modernizes the Baghdad facility. CAAP has cash on hand (more than half a billion dollars as of this latest quarter), and Iraq has a capital city airport that needs significant expansion. This can be a win-win for both sides.

And while Iraq is not at the top of most people's travel wishlists, that doesn't mean the commercial market is tiny. Iraq still has a ton of oil, and there will always be business travel associated with that. In addition, as a national capital, Baghdad is assured of getting plenty of traffic from politicians, diplomats, non-governmental organization workers and so on. Furthermore, I'd note that countries such as Saudi Arabia, U.A.E., and even Kuwait (population: 5 million) have generated robust aviation sectors.

I'm not forecasting that Iraq's airline sector will see that level of success anytime soon, but this could be a much bigger property than investors anticipate. To that point, CAAP is ultimately planning to expand Baghdad's airport to be able to handle 15 million passengers per year. If that capacity comes into use, this would put the airport in a position to be a top five airport within CAAP's overall portfolio.

On the latest conference call, management said that it will give us more concrete information on Baghdad once the final contract is agreed upon and becomes binding. I'll reserve complete judgment until seeing that info. For now, I don't love the deal, but I don't mind it either. CAAP's past M&A has been good, and I'm willing to see how Iraq plays out within the broader portfolio.

CAAP Stock Verdict

Corporacion America Airports continues to add to its business, both with the Iraq deal and several other potential concessions where CAAP is an involved bidder. The firm has a strong balance sheet, with Debt/EBITDA falling below 1.0x recently. I look forward to management deploying its balance sheet at the firm's usual high IRRs to kick off the next leg of the growth story.

In the meantime, core operations are continuing to deliver. The Argentine aviation market, in particular, has been a huge winner in 2025 at a time when so many others have seen significant deceleration. And now, with Milei having a fresh mandate in Argentina, that should pave the way for further deregulation and pro-business measures going forward.

Just look at that Q3 earnings report. The growth story is playing out as expected. And with Argentina giving Milei an electoral mandate in the October vote, that lifts the near-term political overhang that had been holding CAAP shares down. All this sets the stage for this airport operator to continue its move to new highs.

data.title: Alphabet: Why It Beat The Rest Of The Magnificent 7 During The Market Pullback
data.description:
Is This an AI Bubble?

When the market sold off last week on renewed fears of AI bubble and stretched valuations, Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOG:CA, GOOGL) had reached new all-time highs. Over the past four weeks, the stock is up 20% and has been the top performer among the Magnificent 7. So what's behind this bullish momentum?

First, GOOGL's valuation is still pretty reasonable, despite nearly doubling its P/E since the April low. Second, the company delivered a strong 3Q earnings, driven by accelerating AI monetization, and its higher capex has not hurt FCF. Demand for enterprise AI solutions remains robust. I think the recent debut of Gemini 3.0 should only add more momentum. Third, there is a growing speculation on Google chips, which has a potential to challenge Nvidia (NVDA)'s leadership. Therefore, strong fundamentals, reasonable valuation, and bullish sentiment (including positive antitrust outcomes in September) have all contributed to this strong upside momentum.

In my previous analysis, I maintained a Buy on GOOGL because I saw a turnaround opportunity when the stock was lagging its peers and its valuation had contracted below FY2024 levels. The stock had been out of favor as growth slowed. At that time, I pointed out that the market was rewarding AI companies showing strong growth acceleration. GOOGL ended up delivering exactly that in its Q2 and Q3 FY2025 earnings.

Now, the stock is trading at 30x non-GAAP P/E fwd, which still has room for more upside. It may see some near-term consolidation due to its overbought levels. GOOGL has held up extremely well during the recent market selloff. I believe the quality of the stock has improved significantly compared to three months ago. Therefore, GOOGL is still a solid Buy.

Why Google Outperformed META by 35% in One Month

GOOGL's revenue growth started reaccelerating again in Q2 FY2025, driven by advertising revenue (which made up 72.5% of total revenue in the recent quarter). There are many factors that can influence near-term stock sentiment. You might ask: if Meta Platforms' (META) advertising revenue growth accelerated faster than GOOGL's over the last two quarters, why is META's stock down 20% over the past month whereas GOOGL was up 15%?

As I explained in META's recent earnings, a stock's quality largely depends on its bottom-line, such as earnings and FCF. Despite META achieving a stronger top-line growth, its EPS growth slowed to 20% YoY in 3Q FY2025, below GOOGL's 35.4% YoY. Most importantly, GOOGL's FCF over the last 4 quarters (trailing twelve months) grew 32% YoY, while META's FCF instead dropped 15.5% YoY.

Although both companies raised their capex outlooks, I think GOOGL executed far better in cost management while still maintaining a positive FCF growth. From a discounted cash flow standpoint, GOOGL justifies a higher stock quality compared to META.

In addition, META is considering a multi-billion-dollar deal with GOOGL for its TPUs. This news has fueled speculation that GOOGL could challenge NVDA's AI chip leadership, which pushed the stock even higher.

Google Cloud: Scaling AI Monetization and Improving Cost Efficiency

Google Cloud revenue continued accelerating, growing 33.5% YoY in 3Q. However, we also saw both Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS accelerate their revenue growths in the last two quarters, indicating that AI demand remained robust across the hyperscalers. I think what really makes GOOGL stand out is that its elevated capex hasn't hurt its margins, showing strong cost efficiency. Google Cloud's operating margin expanded to 23.7%, up 590 bps compared to 1Q FY2025.

GOOGL does not provide Remaining Performance Obligation (RPO) metrics, which represents total contract backlogs that will be recognized as future revenue. However, management indicated in the earnings call that demand for Enterprise AI infrastructure has been incredibly strong, driven by Gemini 2.5. Cloud backlog reached $155 billion in 3Q, up 46% from the previous quarter. They also noted that the number of +$1 billion deals signed in FY2025 is greater than the total from the past two years combined.

For 4Q FY2025 and FY2026, GOOGL expects a "tight demand-supply environment," especially as the recent release of Gemini 3.0 further boosts AI demand and AI monetization. Given this outlook, I don't see any near-term slowdown for GOOGL.

"Significantly Higher" Capex in FY2026

Even though both META and GOOGL called for significantly higher capex in FY2026, I think GOOGL is in a much better position. First, as mentioned earlier, elevated capex hasn't really pressured its FCF, which is still up 32% YoY on a TTM basis. Second, capex as a percentage of total revenue ratio hasn't increased that much. We can see on the chart that GOOGL's capex has been accelerating this year. In Q3 FY2025, the company raised the F2025 capex outlook to $91 to $93 billion, up from $85 billion guided in Q2. Using the midpoint of $92 billion, we can calculate that its 4Q capex growth would be 99% YoY. The Q4 total revenue consensus is $110.1 billion, which implies the capex ratio would increase to 25.6%.

By contrast, META's capex grew 110.5% YoY in Q3, marking the third consecutive quarter above 100% growth. Moreover, its capex as a percentage of total revenue reached 37.8%, compared to GOOGL's 23.4%. This indicates that META is investing more aggressively in AI infrastructure, raising concerns about potential overspending, as its FCF has suffered from big declines over the past three quarters. More details can be found in my recent META's analysis.

Valuation Is Still Reasonable

On the valuation side, GOOGL is currently overbought as RSI 14 is above 80. This can lead to near-term consolidation. At this level, the stock still looks relatively cheap compared to the broader AI market. GOOGL's GAAP P/E TTM is currently at 31.4x, nearly double from 16.3x in April low. However, the multiple is still cheaper than MSFT's 33.7x and AMZN's 32x. It's also lower than the Nasdaq index's P/E TTM of 32x.

Looking forward, GOOGL trading at 30x non-GAAP P/E fwd still looks reasonable to me, given its scaling AI monetization. Its growth and margin profile is improving. The company still generates FCF, despite maintaining an elevated capex.

This also helps explain why META is trading at a low valuation multiple, at 23.7x non-GAAP P/E fwd, because its aggressive AI investments are pressuring margins and FCF, lowering the stock's quality in the near term.

In addition, GOOGL's multiple expansion can now be lifted by growing optimism around Google chips, which has potential to take market shares from NVDA. META is considering spending billions of dollars on GOOGL's Tensor Processing Unit aka TPUs.

Conclusion

In summary, I don't think GOOGL's 72% rally since my last rating has been largely driven by AI bubble. We see both advertising and cloud revenue have been growing strongly, and improved cost efficiency has significantly pushed margins higher, resulting in stronger earnings growth. Despite higher capex, the company still maintains a positive FCF growth. Google AI chips now have also lifted market sentiment. Even so, trading at 28.5x non-GAAP P/E fwd still looks cheap to me. Therefore, I believe GOOGL remains a high conviction bet: It's a high-quality, AI growth stock with a reasonable valuation, deserving a Buy rating.

data.title: SCHA: A Cost-Efficient, Well-Diversified Small-Cap ETF From Schwab
data.description:
Introduction

The purpose of this article is to introduce readers to the Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF (SCHA), Schwab's low-cost option aimed at investors seeking broad exposure to small capitalization stocks. With a 0.04% expense ratio, $18.7B in assets under management, and a 16-year track record, SCHA is both popular and well-established, and below, you'll find a detailed review of the fund's strategy, its performance, and latest fundamentals compared to other small-cap ETFs. I hope you enjoy the read, and as always, I look forward to your questions afterward.

Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF Overview

Strategy Discussion and Composition

SCHA aims to track the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Small-Cap Total Stock Market Index, whose methodology is linked here. Fortunately, the strategy is simple to understand, as the Index is merely a division of the Dow Jones U.S. Total Stock Market Index that includes securities ranked #751-2500 by total market capitalization, with securities ultimately weighted by their free float market caps. Common stocks and REITs are eligible securities, while other security types, including BDCs, LPs, and ADRs, are not eligible. Lastly, the Index is reconstituted annually in September and rebalanced quarterly.

According to its website, SCHA is currently led by Sandisk (SNDK), Lumentum Holdings (LITE), and Rocket Companies (RKT), though because the size range for small-cap companies is relatively tight, their allocations are small. The top ten holdings below comprise just 4.11% of the fund, which is far less than what you'll find with cap-weighted large-cap funds like the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF (SCHX) - its top ten totals 38.91%.

From a sector allocation perspective, SCHA holds primarily stocks in the Financial (17.83%), Industrial (17.47%), and Technology sectors (15.40%), though most sectors are reasonably well-represented. In addition, roughly two-thirds of the portfolio is allocated to stocks with market caps between $3-$15B, and another 32% to stocks in the $0-$3B range, which many investors might classify as micro-cap.

SCHA Fund Facts and Characteristics

Below is a list of fund facts and characteristics sourced from SCHA's website, its fact sheet, annual financial statements, and quote page on Seeking Alpha.

  • Ticker: SCHA
  • Exchange: NYSE Arca
  • Fund Type: Small Cap Equity
  • Strategy: Passive
  • Benchmark Index: Dow Jones U.S. Small-Cap Total Stock Market Index
  • Inception Date: November 3, 2009
  • Assets Under Management: $18.7B
  • Shares Outstanding: 673,500,000
  • 30 Day Average Daily Volume: 3.79M
  • Expense Ratio: 0.04%
  • 30 Day Median Bid/Ask Spread: 0.04%
  • Premium / Discount: 0.02%
  • 30 Day SEC Yield: 1.31%
  • TTM Distribution Yield: 1.43%
  • Distribution Schedule: Quarterly
  • Portfolio Turnover Rate: 11% (9-15% from 2021-2024)
  • Top Sectors: Financials (18%), Industrials (17%), and Technology (15%)
  • Top 10 Holdings: 4.11%
  • Current Holdings: 1,744

These statistics show SCHA's advantages on cost, liquidity, and diversification, and the ETF also pays a modest 1.43% dividend yield. Although income isn't a primary investment objective, readers should know that due to its ~6% exposure to REITs, not all dividends are considered qualified, and since the yields of REITs are typically quite high, the QDI percentage might be lower than you think. For example, Schwab's Qualified Dividend Income Report for 2024 showed SCHA's figure at just 67.79%. In contrast, QDI percentages for Schwab's large-cap offerings were much higher, since REITs are under-represented in that size segment.

Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF Performance Analysis

SCHA Ten-Year Total Returns vs. Peer Group

Over the last ten years, through October 31, 2025, SCHA has gained 141.24% with reinvested dividends, which puts it in the bottom half of the 22 small-cap blend ETFs I track with a ten-year track record. However, SCHA is part of a cluster of funds with gains of around 140-145%, which includes the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) and the Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 ETF (VIOO). Although SCHA technically ranks #16/22, this table demonstrates its returns have been roughly average over the long run.

SCHA Five-Year Total Returns vs. Peer Group

When expanding the sample size to include small-cap blend ETFs with at least a five-year track record, SCHA's ranking drops to #31/40, delivering a 72.27% total return over that period. Notably, funds with higher fees and similar weighted average market caps outperformed, including the Principal U.S. Small-Cap Multi-Factor ETF (PSC) and the Fidelity Enhanced Small Cap ETF (FESM). Generally, you'll lose some diversification benefits with these multi-factor funds, but they often do a good job filtering out some of the speculative names that find their way into broad-based Index funds like SCHA.

Schwab U.S. Small-Cap ETF Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for SCHA's top 15 holdings, which comprise only 5.54% of the portfolio. Given this concentration, I ask that you focus on SCHA's summary metrics at the bottom and how they compare to its peers, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

SCHA: 1.15 Five-Year Beta Normal For Small-Caps

On a weighted average basis, SCHA's current holdings have a 1.15 five-year beta, indicating above-average market risk that's pretty normal for the small-cap space. It's also reasonably well aligned with the 1.20 beta observed historically, though it's worth noting how SCHX, Schwab's U.S. Large-Cap ETF, has a nearly identical 1.14 weighted average portfolio beta. The reason is that riskier mega-cap growth stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) have substantially greater influence over large-cap indices compared to their average over the last five years, so it's possible that SCHA and SCHX have relatively similar market risk levels at the moment. If accurate, that would be welcome news to SCHA shareholders, since its drawdowns have usually been substantially worse since its inception, as summarized below.

SCHA: Single-Stock Risk High Due To Low Profitability

To be sure, beta only measures market risk, and there are other factors that feed into an ETF's total risk profile. One of them is profitability, and from the fundamental analysis table, it's clear that several holdings have negative EBIT and net margins. Overall, SCHA's EBIT and net margins are 9.73% and 6.49%, respectively, which pale in comparison to SCHX's figures and likely increase single-stock risk. Therefore, small-cap investors should have above-average risk tolerance, and they should be able to tolerate the large drawdowns seen in the earlier table.

From a profitability perspective, SCHA's margins are better than IWM but worse than VIOO, and that's because the S&P SmallCap 600 Index has a positive profitability requirement for new entrants. PSC also features double-digit EBIT and net margins and a solid 8.68% return on total capital, but to reiterate an earlier point, it's less diversified. Readers must decide if they simply want to own the entire small-cap segment with an ETF like SCHA and get close to market-like returns, or if they want to attempt to outperform the market by improving their factor mix at the cost of diversification.

Summary and Conclusion

SCHA's biggest strength is that it's extremely diversified and cost-efficient, given its large 1700+ holdings count, low 4.11% concentration in its top ten holdings, and 0.04% expense ratio, which is almost as cheap as it gets in the small-cap space. Turnover is also consistently low, providing investors with predictability each year, and the ETF also pays a modest dividend of 1.43%, though I remind readers that due to its REIT allocations, only about 68% of the distribution will be classified as QDI.

Readers should also consider the drawbacks of owning the entire small-cap segment. Without the profitability screens employed by S&P SmallCap 600 Index ETFs and most multi-factor funds, SCHA's overall margins end up below those of PSC and VIOO, both of which have outperformed over the last five years. While many long-term investors will be satisfied with receiving the market-like returns SCHA offers, there may be other opportunities for those willing to give up some diversification.

This article was written primarily for educational purposes and, as such, did not include a rating. Instead, my main objective was to answer questions like:

  1. What type of investor is SCHA designed for?
  2. How has SCHA performed against other small-cap ETFs?
  3. What are SCHA's key strengths and weaknesses?

I hope I have answered the above questions, but if anything is unclear or you are interested in learning more about the alternatives to SCHA, please let me know in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Energy Transfer Is A Better Opportunity By Each Passing Quarter
data.description:
Introduction

A lot has happened for Energy Transfer (NYSE: ET) over the past couple of months, one of my principal holdings, as well as probably the company I have mostly followed. With a blend of quarterly earnings and new partnerships, I believe that now could be a great time for the company, looking forward despite falling commodity prices.

As a reminder to readers, ET is a major midstream company in the country that has a presence in all of the energy value chain. With its mostly fee-based model, it is relatively stable and has gained from being less directly exposed to volatile commodity prices.

Current Dynamics

First, ET released its Q3 earnings with a slight decrease in Adj. EBITDA at $3.84B, down from $3.96B Y/Y. The same trend was seen for the company’s overall DCF, with a drop to $1.9B down from $1.99B Y/Y. Though it is important to note that without some non-recurring items, such as some tax accrual adjustments, Adj. EBITDA is overall flat compared to the previous year despite a weaker environment for ET, thus highlighting the strength of the core fee-based business. This strength is why ET was confident in increasing its distribution by more than 3% to $0.3325 per unit. And though some investors could be worried about this kind of increase, I believe that it is fine, as the coverage ratio still sits comfortably over 2x.

Looking at segment performance, we can directly see that NGLs have been the best-performing part of ET’s portfolio, and that was thanks to record export and transportation volumes. Interstate Natural Gas was, however, slightly down, mainly from lower transportation volumes on the Bakken Pipeline, while intrastate was relatively strong thanks to the high-demand market, such as Texas. These two segments are particularly interesting to follow as ET has dedicated the bulk of its growth CapEx towards natural gas as it is expected that it will be the main growth driver for the midstream company as the world relies more and more on the transition fuel.

Regarding the guidance, ET is expecting Adj. EBITDA to come in at the lower end of the spectrum of $16.1B to $16.5B which would still be the highest recorded by the partnership.

Outside of the recent earnings, ET surprised many when it directly inked a deal with Oracle and Voltagrid, putting it at the forefront of the AI energy-driven narrative. ET will supply natural gas for three Oracle AI data centers, which directly puts ET at the forefront of this industry. And even as I have my doubts about Oracle’s current prospects as I mentioned in my analysis of the company, I believe that this is a net positive for ET with a deep cooperation with both Oracle and Voltagrid, highlighting how important midstream companies will be with the rise of energy demand. Furthermore, this all ties with the long-term, fee-based contracts motto of the company, and I also believe that it will reduce some of the cyclicality, as it will pull away from traditional refineries and petrochemical plants that are more dependent on the economic cycle. It will also translate into a high utilization of existing assets of both intrastate and interstate pipeline systems, which will lead to higher margin revenue by operating underutilized capacity. ET will also construct eight 10-megawatt natural gas-fired electric generation facilities that will support its own operations in Texas, thus increasing efficiency and reducing reliance on the grid.

On another note, ET is continuing to expand on its coastal assets to remain a leader in NGL exports, which is set to have around 20% of the worldwide market share for all NGL exports. The Nederland Flexport expansion will add up to 250,000 Bbls/d and the Mont Belvieu Frac IX will also add 165,000 Bbls/d fractionation capacity, coming online by Q4 2026.

Valuation

Overall, I still believe that the company is the biggest bargain in the midstream market, especially as highlighted by its valuation metrics. ET still has a peer low EV/EBITDA of 8.98, far below the closest peers such as EPD at 11.11 or Enbridge at 15.08, and I believe that ET could take away from ENB’s mainline with ETCOP and DAPL. Furthermore, the company is generating great FCF yields, with the second-highest among its peers at 9.1%, though it is at the lower end of the average FCF yield of the past 5 years. On the distribution front, that is one of the biggest investment points for income investors, ET is gaining back compared to the past couple of years, with a forward yield of 8.06%, over peers such as MPLX that used to have a higher yield in the past.

With each passing quarter, I’m more and more bullish on the growth prospects of the company. The Oracle partnership is again one of those measures that tells us the sheer grasp the company has over the energy supply chain, where it can see an opportunity and directly cater its infrastructure network to serve it. Another aspect would be the current push with the Lake Charles LNG project; even with the FID being delayed to Q1 2026, it is still on track to sell down 80% of the project equity to dilute the risk before proceeding. And as mentioned earlier, the company is focusing its CapEx towards Natural Gas, which is the right move in my opinion, with projects such as the Desert Southwest pipeline and the Hugh Brinson Pipeline that I’ve covered in my previous analysis.

With the sheer size of ET, we’ve covered that margins are not the greatest for the company due to its vast size and its approach of serving all types of energy. Yet with the Oracle deal and potential future related deals, I could see them boosting the utilization rate and therefore margins for ET. This has pushed me to revise the overall future margins that I’m expecting for the midstream company, and I believe we could see EBITDA margins upwards of 22% with full realizations. On the return on equity side, ET has proven to be a strong capital allocator over time as it shifted away from pricey acquisitions and doubled down on internal growth projects. I welcomed this shift, as I believe that it helped the partnership improve overall margins over the past 10 years, with EBITDA margins moving from 11.68% back in 2015 to now 18.55%.

Risks

Though ET derives around 90% of its EBITDA from fee-based sales, it is important to note that these are still affected by indirect price exposure through simple volumes. The current fall in commodity prices has pushed producers/customers to slow down their production, which should, in effect, reduce pass-through volumes, thus overall revenue for Energy Transfer.

And on the natural gas side, which is currently the most productive segment, it is generating the bulk of its profit from its marketing operation. Thus, the tight capacity out of the Permian has helped ET, but this trend could reverse, eating away at the profitable operation.

Conclusion

I believe that right now, ET is still the greatest opportunity in the midstream sector, especially now that it is securing great deals that will push the partnership forward. It is facing sector-wide weaknesses, yet this has pushed its distribution yield at a highly fair level for the associated risk. Blended together, ET still has the lowest valuation metrics in the industry, while it has the best road ahead as the company’s tentacles are in the whole energy supply chain. I keep my rating at a Strong Buy for Energy Transfer.

data.title: Unum Group: A "Hold" In 2025
data.description:
Sometimes it's fun to go back and see what companies have made of themselves. I was once a major investor in Unum (UNM), and I will show you here why it can be argued that I was perhaps too early to exit my investment in the company. However, it's worth recalling that I made a 100%+ RoR in less than 24 months in this investment, and was one of the very few investors calling for "BUY" when the company was down in the dumps way back when. I was the only SA analyst to give Unum a "STRONG BUY" rating back in 2020, and the results for that investment up until now look like this.

Of course, this return is only valid if you held for all that time. I did not, and I doubt there are many that did. I make the point here because I want to show you that the core consideration of any investment, together with quality and dividends, is the valuation of what you're buying. If you're buying quality at an A-valuation, you can get returns like this, in what I consider to be record time. We're talking about 5-6 years - not exactly a long time for more than tripling your invested money.

Now, as mentioned, I did not get 250%+. My RoR was closer to 150% inclusive of dividends, but I sold all the way back in 2023. Since then, the return on the capital I reinvested elsewhere has outpaced the return that Unum has garnered since then. So I still consider this to be a solid investment, and a "right" choice, even if it's indisputably an excellent choice to have waited until now.

However, I believe, as we're seeing now, and as the financial industry is looking, the time has most certainly come to sell things off here, or to "rotate" if you will.

I will show you why exactly that is in this article, and what you could end up buying instead - though you'll have a hard time finding a good deal as UNM here.

I remind you also that Unum's yield is less than 2.5% here. My own YoC was over 7% on my position - again due to valuation.

Let's look at what's happened here.

Unum - Significant Improvement since my last piece in 2023

You're able to find my last piece in the article here, from over 2 years ago now. The reason I haven't updated on the company is quite simple. I considered the company "done" in terms of my thesis. It was out of my portfolio, and I was very happy with the return, and moved on to my next target. That's how I invest. I kept Unum in my watchlist, followed its earnings updates, but did not reinvest.

The company kept "silent" for the most part until late 2024/early 2025, when a significant bounce happened due to operational appeal. The reason I'm updating now is that SA analysts have the company at "BUY", and Wall Street averages are at "BUY" too. I don't quite agree with the SA Quant rating of "SELL" here, but it is definitely not a "BUY" to me.

I therefore consider Unum worthy of an update here.

Since my last update, the L&H (that's life and health) insurance company Unum has done very well for itself. Since its decline due to, among other things, LTC issues, the company has excelled at capital allocation. Recent reinsurance deals (much like Lincoln National, by the way), have reduced the LTC block risks in a most appealing manner at a comparatively low cost, which has unlocked provisioned capital for new investments.

Again, though, these are things I mentioned several years ago as upsides. These are not new. Much like other L&H, LTC remains an issue here, and also will likely remain an issue for some time yet to come - but anyone calling Unum fundamentally dangerous, I would disagree with that. Like LNC (but better), Unum has excellent capital ratios and strong operational safety. The focus that Unum has on its LTC issues can be seen in company updates.

The upside Unum has been seeing here is related, in my view, to execution in the LTC business more than other business operation success. By company calculations, the Fortitude Re transaction removes 19% of LTC reserves, next to 20% of premiums, which was a $100M capital benefit when it was decided.

Since my last article on the company back in -23, UNM has seen strong financial results. In 2024 YoY to 2023, the latest full-year results, the company saw a double-digit EBIT increase, with a strong EPS increase. Premium income was up 4.5% in the same timeframe, and while this was below European peers for the most part, it's still decent. Throughout all of it, Unum has maintained very solid capital ratios. Unlike LNC, it never went fully negative, and with a BBB rating, its creditworthiness has never really been in question.

Unum is a very solid life & health insurance business. Based on the latest 3Q25 results, the company is likely to see long-term growth. However, the question is at what level. I currently forecast 2025E results to be either flat or negative, with my own EPS forecast at $8.3/share (adjusted), and growth potentially in 2026 from premium growth and some of these efficiencies coming in more clearly. The fact that the Unum holding company has liquidity now of $2B should be enough to dissuade anyone uncertain about the company's safety.

However, that doesn't mean that there isn't risk, as my forecast for the year should show you. I'm pointing to things that are already visible for the US segment, which saw a 3Q25 operating decline of 7.8% for the quarter, 14.8% in the group disability line, which was mostly related to assumption updates. Remember, this is how the insurance industry works (and it's why these companies tendentially trade at low multiples, due to the volatility potential when something does not turn out right).

Because aside from the focus on LTC reinsurance (which I think we can at least partially move on from now). Instead, what someone who works with analyzing insurance, such as me, looks at is things like the significant actuarial justified rate increases, likely related to the aforementioned LTC block assumptions, and what the company's assumptions about morbidity and co-morbidity are. The fact that the company is raising rates so significantly means that prior to the premium hikes, these policies were operating at a loss. However, it's worth remembering that rate increases like this are subject to regulatory approval.

The disability trends are also worth noting. Management got a question about them in the earnings call, but considers them not to be not worrying and within expectations. I would take that with a bit more salt, and expect more volatility there (benefit ratios at 62% are decent, but I'd err on the conservative side there). Because among L&H companies, many are reporting trends in disability not being as strong as expected, which was again cited in the call as well from one of the participants.

I suppose what I want to convey is that there is a reason these companies trade at 5-8x P/E, and expecting them to go higher comes at the risk of volatility.

Which is why I don't.

Valuation is as follows.

Valuation for Unum - Not that attractive here

Unum tends towards a multiple of 5-8x, sometimes higher, sometimes lower. I would put it at about that level, with 5.8x as the sort of lowest potential. The problem is that this implies a fair value of below $60/share for the period of 2027E, which comes to a negative RoR of 15% for the next few years.

The fact is, even if you expect the company to trade at 7x P/E, or a fair value of $72/share, the RoR is still negative, if only barely.

What I seek to explain or show to you here is the risk you take by investing at this price. If you believe a 7x P/E is not possible, all you need do is look a few months back, or a year or more back, when the company traded close to 5.8x.

I update my price target and fair value target for Unum, as before, based on a book value basis. The current book value of the company is around $65/share, or just below. I see most of the analysts currently following Unum citing upside based on the LTC considerations. I say that there are far more components for downside risks than those, and for those who know what to look for, the risks in the company's operations (and other insurance companies as well) are becoming clearer and clearer. It's too far to say that "all insurance companies are overvalued". In some scenarios, I could call Allstate (ALL) undervalued - but I would consider Unum to be overvalued here. And to those who say that analyst targets are X, let me remind you what those analyst averages were back during COVID-19, when I invested.

By following analyst averages, it's my view that you don't do your portfolios any favors. I've found that these averages tend to track share prices more than contrarian convictions. A valid criticism raised against me is that I have a tendency to sell my winners too early - that's something I'm working on.

But in this case, I do not believe Unum to be worth $90+ per share, because I do not believe a P&C insurance with this history and risk profile is deserving of such a book value premium (close on 36%).

I would take the conservative road - a fair value according to the book, at $65/share, with a PT of $50/share or below.

Alternatives? I would consider Lincoln National, even if that company cannot be considered massively cheap here either - but at least it has a 15%+ annualized upside.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Consideration

The primary risk to Unum is obviously not quality here, but one of valuation. At the current valuation, I do not see an upside that, based on either historical data or averages, could see a market-beating rate of return. In fact, based on those same historical averages, I see a potential for capital loss if you were to invest here, based on high multiples and generally unattractive market heights.

Operational challenges here are few, but they do exist. Unum's troubles didn't go as deep as Lincoln National's (LNC), but I believe the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings capture the issues well, citing growth, profitability, and revision issues, focusing on a valuation issue. With cash flow multiples as high as we're seeing here, and growth metrics seeing significant challenges, I do not believe Unum should be valued highly here.

Together with LNC, the LTC blocks are a risk, and these will continue to weigh on returns here. I believe the market is severely underestimating the impact of these trends, and would call the company an unattractive "HOLD" here, bordering on a "SELL" (though I tend to mostly use SELL ratings for fundamentally unattractive or problematic businesses, which Unum decidedly is not).

Thesis

Unum is a theoretically appealing insurance company, but it no longer has a sufficiently attractive upside in terms of valuation. Because of this, I cannot in good conscience rate the company anything but a "HOLD" after 2-3 years of silence on the company. I will endeavor to update this rating more frequently.

In a show of the validity of valuation, I am also not shifting my long-term fair value and PT by much. I consider the company fairly valued at $64/share, around book value, with a PT of around $48/share, making today's valuation significantly overvalued, despite some good performances.

Unum is a "HOLD" with a PT of $45/share, and I would still consider other investments significantly above Unum at this valuation and upside.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $48/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2025.

data.title: Kinross Gold: Positioned Perfectly For A Repriced Gold Market (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Despite being up ~60% since I first covered Kinross Gold (KGC) back in July, I believe the stock still represents a great opportunity to diversify a portfolio’s gold exposure.

With gold prices still standing strong near their all-time high and what I’d call changing fundamentals for the entire industry that could support higher prices going forward, KGC remains a low-cost, healthy gold mining option to buy, and I’ll actually upgrade them to a Strong Buy as they’re proving to be even better than expected.

Internal Developments

As we can see, KGC reported a massive jump in FCF once again, nearly doubling the 9M’24 level of $917.1 million with $1.79 billion reported over the first 9 months of 2025, of which $711.9 million came in Q3 alone despite the higher CAPEX this quarter. Assuming another $700 million in Q4 if gold prices continue to hover around these levels (and the same CAPEX), we’re looking at nearly $2.5 billion in FCF in 2025, which is still great for a $29.43 billion market cap.

KGC also increased its dividend by 17% in early November, although the forward yield is still very small (~0.57%), which is not uncommon for miners. Still, that’s about $167.75 million per year, so it’s not a lot of money given how much they can make. This comes on top of the restarted buyback program (April 2025), buying back about $405 million as of November and increasing their target by 20% and aiming to repurchase $600 million in 2025, for a combined yield of ~2.6%.

The company’s production is expected to remain relatively stable at ~2 million gold equivalent ounces through 2027, with stable attributable CAPEX of ~$1.15 billion per year, +/- 5%. To be honest, as much as I like this, I believe this comes with a weaker valuation in the market’s eyes since there’s little growth - at least in the near-term.

As you can see, KGC has quite a few big projects such as Lobo-Marte (Chile), Maricunga (Chile) or Great Bear (Canada) with potential to impact their cash flows especially after 2030 and currently in various stages of early development, while also having potential to extend current operations into the 2030s. Especially since I expect higher gold prices going forward, I can definitely see them continuing to focus on all of these, especially now since they have significant capital and no near-term debt, as I mention below.

Financially, based on KGC’s latest report, we see an excellent position, with the current assets nearly covering their total liabilities, with a ton of cash on hand and little debt. This offers a lot of flexibility, and I’d prefer if they focused more on developing their projects rather than paying the very small dividend or doing buybacks at elevated prices - especially since there are other options to buy if they really want to buy something.

In fact, KGC recently even redeemed all of their $500 million 4.50% senior notes due in July 2027, being left with $750 million in notes outstanding, with the next maturity date on July 2033 ($500 million). This places them in an excellent position, and I believe they can now focus on using their cash flows for expanding more rather than fortifying its balance sheet and current mines, as I’ll mention below.

I wouldn’t be surprised to see them talk about a “new era” for the company now that debt is nowhere near an issue (net cash now) and they’re making so much money, but we’ll see what they say in their Q4 report when we also potentially get new guidance for next year.

Macro Developments

Of course, the main factor behind KGC’s performance has been the unprecedented jump in gold prices, continuing to remain very strong (still very volatile), with the recent peak at nearly $4,400 per ounce meaning it doubled in a couple of years and quadrupled in less than 10.

This is a monumental jump for what’s considered a slow and boring commodity, but I believe it makes a lot of sense and is more than justified nowadays given the environment we’re in. Simply put, all the global uncertainty, US rate cuts likely advancing in the near-term despite the US Government going through its longest shutdown in history (ended recently), the unpredictable situation around Venezuela, AI overspending worries, uncertainty around tariffs, and many other factors ultimately support a high level of fear, which caused what I believe to be a delayed reaction that comes as the market can’t ignore the need for a hedge anymore.

And again, compared to other precious metals, gold’s potential is nearly unlimited as a result of their sources of demand (see above), with the current revaluation making sense after years of it being ignored while its fundamentals continued to advance, as I mention below, all while the market focused on pre-pandemic/stay-at-home hype stocks, AI optimism (which is now starting to fade), etc.

Other precious metals like silver or platinum are ultimately “capped” by significantly higher demand coming from real-life uses, while investing/banks/jewelry take much smaller shares in their total demand. Gold relies on these 3 main sources of demand however, which aren’t subject to the normal supply-demand based market dynamics, while its “normal” supply source (technology) takes a very, very small portion of the demand for gold. For that one, higher prices can easily result in lower demand and the industry seeking replacements.

However, unlike platinum’s main source (catalytic converters used in the auto industry) or silver’s electronics/solar panels/etc., gold’s other sources of demand aren’t really subject to this type of market dynamics. Jewelry for example is bought for its preciousness, not how good of a deal we get when buying it (it’s a desire with inelastic demand, not a necessity), while investing and banks are clearly subject to hype, FOMO or other market phenomena, which is why this commodity has a nearly uncapped potential in my opinion.

Another thing to remember is that not even the previous jump in gold prices to over $2,000 per ounce was enough to lead to a significant increase in production, while most gold miners instead went for consolidation (mergers or acquisitions, which are still happening) and some organic improvements or even diversification into metals like copper, barely replacing the depleted resources. Again, keep in mind that even if they wanted to, opening a gold mine can take many years even if we don’t count the exploration stage, with permitting and feasibility often taking years on top of construction and eventually ramping up the production to the peak planned potential.

Still, one thing to note is that the currently exceptional gold prices could also provide significant capital for miners to invest and also improve the feasibility of many already-known projects with failed feasibility studies because of production costs (nobody would touch a $2,500 break-even project with gold at $2,000, but $4,000 is another story), but things still won’t happen overnight. And even if these higher-AISC projects become operational, that also means that the median AISC will continue to grow and support higher prices in the long-term, while falling below $2,000 for example could make significant parts of the production unreliable.

One thing that can lead to higher supply in the near-term could be the generally lower-grade Chinese mines that can now keep producing or even ramp-up, making their large-scale operations even more competitive on a global scale.

As you can see, gold’s fundamentals continued to change drastically especially from 2020 onwards, which is a reason why I believe the current re-pricing is rational. The median AISC jumped significantly, reaching over $1,500 per ounce recently (as of Q2; Q3 data not available yet) and shifting the metal’s fundamentals completely compared to even less than a decade ago.

Nowadays, gold getting to $2,000 per ounce for example would lower today’s production by about ~15% in terms of sustainable AISC production, while a drop to a bit over $1,500 basically makes nearly half of it unprofitable, which is a monumental difference compared to not even a decade ago.

If we think back about a decade ago when gold fell even below $1,100 per ounce, that level was actually realistic given the market’s dynamics at the time, as the median AISC was even below $900 per ounce (see the previous image). Back then, this drop to $1,100 still meant that over half of the production was still profitable in terms of AISC, with gold at roughly 4 times lower levels than the recent records.

Meanwhile, if that $1,100 came today, we’d be looking at nearly 90% of the production being unsustainable, which is realistically impossible to see even if some miners would adapt and adjust costs, leading to detrimental market disruptions.

Still, that doesn’t mean gold prices can’t fall. Even though I’m bullish on the metal in the long-term, near-term volatility can always push KGC’s stock price and its profitability lower, while their current and future mines come with some political risk since they’re located in what the market considers riskier jurisdictions.

Updated Valuation

Given their proven performance, I believe we can see KGC produce about $2.5 billion in FCF in 2025 if we assume realized gold prices of about $3,300 per ounce (vs. the average realized gold prices of $3,200 per ounce during 9M’25), with flat CAPEX. I’ll assume a CAGR of 4% for the entire 10-year period thanks to the first 5 years benefiting from rate cuts and debt repayments and post-2027 investments, followed by their potential to expand with the new projects, and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a WACC of 8.75% (above my estimated 8.1%; assumptions: ~8.3% cost of equity, ~4.6% cost of debt) with $499 million in net cash now, $126 million in noncontrolling interests and 1.22 billion shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $44.55 billion, an equity value of $44.92 billion, and an intrinsic value of $36.82 per share.

Conclusions

I’m upgrading KGC to a Strong Buy given their constant overperformance, still very attractive valuation, improved financials and solid long-term potential. There are some risks tied mostly to the commodity’s volatility, but the current level remains attractive even if the price of gold drops, while gold’s fundamentals are also changing and potentially supporting higher prices going forward.

data.title: October's Treasury Statement: Affects Of The Shutdown On Deficit
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this week, the US Treasury Department released the Monthly Treasury Statement for the month of October. The monthly Treasury statement details revenue and expenditures for the US government and debt activity with respect to the issuance of Treasury bonds. The October statement is usually boring as it is the first month of the new fiscal year, but this year’s results came during a government shutdown. The data from the October report details the impact of the shutdown on government finances and some other realities with respect to our deficit.

Overall Revenue/Expense/Deficit Picture

The most obvious question people have about the shutdown is whether the government closing helped the deficit. In short, the answer is no. Revenue collection was certainly not impaired by a lack of operations. The government collected nearly $217 billion in individual income taxes during the month of October, a nearly $50 billion increase from October of 2024. While corporate income taxes and social security taxes rose nominally, the other big increase was in customs duties, from $7 billion last October to $31 billion this year, mainly due to the tariffs.

On the expense side, several departments saw notable price declines due to the government shutdown. The Department of Agriculture, Department of Education, Department of Homeland Security, Department of Justice, and Department of State were all among the departments that saw major year over year spending declines.

Despite the drop in expenses in each of those departments, huge spending increases from the Department of Health and Human Services, interest on debt, the Department of Veteran Affairs, and the Social Security Administration caused the total government spending to surge. For the month of October, the government ended up spending more than $688 billion compared to $584 billion in October 2024, a $104 billion or nearly 18% increase. In the end, the government deficit for the month of October was $284 billion compared to $257 billion in 2024. While we still have data for November to report, it is likely that the shutdown did nothing to help the deficit.

More Details into Spending Growth

Despite the growth in the government’s income, government spending continues to outpace the growth in revenue. While government spending, on a trailing twelve-month basis, is flat at around $7 trillion today compared to 2021, it is a far cry from the $4.5 trillion that was spent on a trailing twelve-month basis prior to the pandemic.

Our current government spending is heavily concentrated on defense, entitlements, and interest payments. As of October, 77% of total government spending comprised of defense, Medicare/Medicaid, interest, and Social Security in the last 12 months. Except for pandemic era spending, the “big four” has contributed to over 70% of total spending for some time, with a recent bump to over 75% of total spending.

Social Security and Medicare/Medicaid account for nearly half of all government spending and there appears to be no end in sight. CMS spending is on pace to reach $2 trillion on a trailing twelve-month basis during fiscal year 2026, which is double fiscal year 2016 levels. Social Security will likely cross the $1.5 trillion trailing twelve-month mark during fiscal year 2026 and will also be double the fiscal year 2016 levels.

While Social Security is encapsulated in a trust, the trust is rapidly liquidating. The Social Security trust started running deficits in 2018, and after a brief surplus from 2019 through 2021, has seen a deepening deficit since. In the trailing twelve months ending in October, the Social Security trust has drained nearly $200 billion from reserves. While the balance of the reserve is significant, investors also need to look at the flow as significant due to its signaling of the federal government selling treasury bonds, which is bearish to long term debt prices.

Can Tariffs Bail Us Out?

There’s been hope that the increase in tariff revenue could help offset government expense increases. While tariff revenue has risen, it is not at levels set to make a material impact. On a trailing twelve-month basis, custom duties revenue came in at over $200 billion in the month of October.

Based on the monthly data, customs revenues should come in at around $350 billion on a trailing twelve-month basis by next summer, with the possibility of rising close to $400 billion after that. Despite what would represent a nearly $300 billion increase in revenue, that level of tariff revenue would support approximately 4% of total government spending. In fact, the increase in tariff revenue would only cover a quarter of the current level of interest expense on the national debt.

Conclusion

Without broad entitlement reform, higher taxes, lower interest rates, or some combination of the three, the United States spending and deficit woes will continue. The likely default if nothing changes will force the Federal Reserve to monetize debt and fuel inflation further with respect to consumer prices. Ultimately, the government shutdown will end up a distant footnote of no consequence in the pages of history.

data.title: EverCommerce: Revenue Clouds Darken Amid Positive Earnings And Share Buybacks (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Outlook

EverCommerce (EVCM) provides a range of field service software and commerce capabilities to various industries.

I previously analyzed EVCM in October 2024 with a Sell outlook due to slowing growth and reduced cost-cutting efforts.

While management’s capital allocation moves are helpful and the company has recently produced positive earnings, revenue growth is turning negative.

Since its IPO in 2021, the stock has performed poorly and until the firm can produce meaningful overall revenue growth, my outlook is a bearish Sell.

EverCommerce’s Market And Approach

EverCommerce in the field service and commerce market, focusing on specific verticals including home services, health service providers and the wellness service industry.

According to a 2025 market research report by MarketsAndMarkets, the global market for field service management software was estimated at $5.1 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $9.2 billion by 2030.

If accomplished, this would represent a CAGR of a robust 12.5% from 2026 to 2030.

This demand growth is expected to come from continued innovation by vendors, growing cloud adoption by clients and the ongoing integration of generative AI and machine learning technologies to enhance field decision-making and processing.

The chart below shows the expected growth path by region, with the Asia-Pacific region projected to grow at the highest rate through 2030:

Major competitors or industry players include the following:

  • ServiceTitan (TTAN)
  • Salesforce (CRM)
  • Housecall Pro
  • FieldEdge
  • Tebra
  • Athenahealth
  • NextGen
  • Mindbody
  • Zenoti
  • Vagaro
  • Manual processes
  • Point solution competitors

EVCM sells its solutions via a typical ‘land and expand’ approach common for primarily subscription-based software companies. It seeks to begin in one area or unit of a company (‘land’) and ‘expand’ into other segments or units as EVCM proves the value of its offerings.

The revenue contribution percentages by industry segment and type are shown in the pie chart here, with Home and Health Services accounting for 95% by segment, Subscriptions at 82.2% and Payments at 14.2% by type:

The industry features various types of players, from large horizontal platforms that want to become the ‘operating system’ for field service across all industries, such as Service Titan and Salesforce, to smaller industry-focused ‘micro-aggregators’ that share a common back-end payment infrastructure like EverCommerce and Simpro.

A third group in the industry would be ‘consumer grade’ growth tools, like those offered by Housecall Pro, FieldEdge and Jobber, to bring features like marketing automation and dynamic quoting to small, mom-and-pop companies.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary market characteristics:

Strengths

  • Vertical specialization, sharpened focus by recent divestitures, integrated payments and large customer base of SMBs

Weaknesses

  • Brand fragmentation through acquisitions, integration complexity and cost, reliance on SMBs with higher churn during economic stress

Opportunities

  • Agentic and generative AI technology enhancements, payments cross-selling, M&A deals, hybrid healthcare models

Threats

  • Intense competition from established players, labor shortages in service end markets

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has risen YoY on higher payments revenue and core SaaS revenue growth. Operating income by quarter (red line) has risen because of stable margins and operating costs against higher revenue.

The degree of operating leverage trend has remained relatively stable, though negative.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has varied within a range since 2024, while Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have remained stable due to cost optimization efforts. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have also proceeded with no discernible trend.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have produced two quarters of positive results, an encouraging trend.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, EVCM ended the quarter with $106.9 million in cash and equivalents and $523.8 million in total debt, all of which was long-term.

In the past 12 months, EVCM’s stock price has fallen by 29% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF’s (IGV) slide of 3%, with the difference primarily since its Q3 earnings announcement, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a helpful financial and operating metrics table for EVCM is shown here:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

3.3

EV/EBITDA - FWD

11.0

Rev. Growth Rate Est. - FWD

-2.8%

2025 FWD EPS Estimate

$0.59

Price/Sales - TTM

2.1

P/E GAAP - TTM

NM

Revenue Growth Rate - TTM

22.1%

Net Income Margin - TTM

-0.1%

EBITDA Margin - TTM

17.6%

Market Capitalization

$1,500,000,000

Enterprise Value

$1,940,000,000

Operating Cash Flow

$138,600,000

EPS (Fully Diluted)

-$0.01

Free Cash Flow per Share - TTM

$0.61

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 2.63

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance has improved markedly from the middle of 2024:

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was an impressive $140.8 million, and capital expenditures were just $2.2 million.

The company paid a reasonable $28.4 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was $128.4 million.

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $74.5 million, so the company has been repurchasing its stock at a meaningful rate.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 4.6%, a relatively low figure.

Also, the Board authorized a $50 million increase in share buybacks, with the total authorization being $300 million through the end of 2026.

Overall, the company is executing on a ‘shrink the float’ approach by using a large percentage of its free cash flow to buy its shares, increasing free cash flow and earnings per share in a shareholder-friendly focus.

Why I’m Bearish On EverCommerce

EverCommerce will likely see overall revenue contraction in 2025 due to a confluence of rebate softness, legacy product headwinds and general usage-based revenue volatility.

The company has recently acquired ZyraTalk for its AI receptionist, scheduling and dispatch capabilities, but also for EVCM’s plan to integrate its agentic AI features across its platform, adding value to other systems within its broader portfolio.

Presumably, this will enhance efficiencies and product differentiation in forward-focused clients and prospects.

While revenue may be challenged in the near term, GAAP margins have been stable with the opportunity for growth in adjusted EBITDA from its ongoing cost optimization efforts and a shift toward a higher-margin revenue mix.

Management’s current growth strategy is to increase the ‘attach rate’ of clients using its payment processing features, integrating AI and ML capabilities across its portfolio and cross-selling to increase multiple solution usage, which improves share of wallet and customer retention.

To this end, while the current net dollar retention rate is only 97%, customers using more than one solution have demonstrated an NDRR of over 100%, a positive confirming development.

I’ve created a graphic illustrating the frequency of various negative keywords used in the most recent conference call between management and analysts:

The chart indicates that the company is experiencing challenges with tariffs affecting part of its customer base and macroeconomic effects on rebates.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing EVCM at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.3x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of negative (2.8%) against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. EVCM’s NDRR of 97%, so the company has performed poorly against the benchmark.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 4.9x on October 31, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, EVCM is currently being valued by public investors at a discount to the broader index as of October 31, 2025, although the discount is less than I would have otherwise expected, likely due to its strong free cash flow generation.

Looking ahead, with a continued strong share repurchase program and the prospect for higher margins and AI integration momentum, the share price may have reached a floor, although the recent drop immediately after the earnings report likely signals investor disappointment with revenue growth stalling.

Since its IPO in 2021, the stock has mostly traded downward, and I don’t see any major forward catalyst for the stock, despite recent positive earnings growth uncoupled from its revenue outlook.

So, investor capital would probably be better deployed elsewhere.

My outlook on EVCM is downgraded to a bearish Sell.

data.title: Broadcom Q4 Earnings: 2 Accounting Details To Watch For
data.description:
AVGO stock: Q4 earnings expected on December 11

My last coverage on Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) stock was published on October 14 in an article titled "Broadcom's OpenAI Deal: You Should Consider Covered Call Options.” The coverage focused on the co-development deal with OpenAI (OPENAI) the company announced at that time. It rated the stock as a Hold and suggested the use of covered call options. Looking ahead, a key catalyst on the horizon is the company’s earnings report (ER) for Q4 2025, schedule for release on Dec 11.

Given the company’s elevated P/E ratios and also the market’s anxiety over the current state of AI investments, we feel it could be timely to share our thoughts on the upcoming ER. In particular, we will focus on two accounting details: the treatment of Restructuring and Integration Costs and also Stock-Based Compensation (SBC). As to be elaborated later, we feel these treatments could make the company’s valuation risks even higher than what’s on the surface, thus offsetting its strong business fundamentals.

As you can see from the release of Broadcom’s Q3 2025 ER below, there is a large divergence between the standard accounting and normalized earnings. The chasm between its GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) earnings and its non-GAAP “adjusted” figures is quite massive in my view. Its GAAP diluted EPS was just $0.85, while its non-GAAP EPS was nearly double at $1.69. Looking ahead to the Q4 earnings, the gap between the GAAP and normalized EPS estimates remains sizable. As seen, consensus anticipates a GAAP EPS of $1.12 and a non-GAAP EPS of $1.87.

Next, I will dissect 2 accounting details and explain why the normalized EPS may represent an overestimation of its true earnings power.

AVGO stock: Restructuring and Integration Costs

The first primary driver of Broadcom's above earnings discrepancy is related to its external acquisitions in my analyses. Broadcom has been a serial acquirer in recent years (with the recent acquisition and integration of VMware as a notable example). Due to these acquisitions, the company carries billions of dollars in "intangible assets" (like patents, customer lists, and technology) on its balance sheet and also reports large amounts of restructuring and integration costs.

To illustrate the magnitude of these costs, the chart below shows its operating expenses from its latest income statements. As seen, the company reported a total of more than $1.52 billion under “amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets” for the first 9 months of 2025 and more than $2.43 billion for the first 9 months of 2024. Similarly, the company also reported a total of about $0.44 billion under “Restructuring and other charges” for the first 9 months of 2025 and more than $1.22 billion for the first 9 months of 2024. These expenses are a significant portion of its total operating expenses (of around $13 and $14 billion for these periods).

To obtain the non-GAAP and normalized EPS, these costs were often considered "one-time" events and thus are added back to the earnings. However, given the magnitude and also the historical track record of these costs, I remain skeptical if such costs are indeed “one-time.” Especially if you subscribe to my view that external acquisition has been and will likely continue to be an integral part of the AVGO’s growth strategy. Under this view, acquisition and integration effectively become a recurring operational expense.

AVGO stock: Stock-Based Compensation

The next large adjustment on my list is SBC (Stock-Based Compensation). In its reconciliation, Broadcom excludes the cost of issuing stock to employees from its non-GAAP earnings. Technically, issuing stocks is a non-cash expense for the company. However, I believe SBC is a very real economic cost to shareholders if the issuance is sizable and also recurring.

And unfortunately, this seems to be case for AVGO judging by its cash flow statements as shown below. Also, AVGO’s SBC has shown a rapid increase over the years. The reported SBC more than tripled in the past ~2 years or so between end of 2023 and the present (from $2.17 billion in Oct 2023 to more than $6.68 as of TTM). In 2024, Broadcom reported an SBC adjustment of $5.74 billion as seen, almost equal to its reported net income of $5.89 billion.

In my model, such sizable and recurring use of SBC in essence is a transfer of value from shareholders to AVGO employees by inflating the share count. As such, from the perspective of AVGO investors, the non-GAAP EPS figures and/or the operating cash flows after the SBC adjustment represent an overestimating the company’s profitability.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of the upside risks, I am optimistic about the company’s business fundamentals and long-term growth potential for the reasons detailed in my last article. As a reflection of its growth potential, the chart below shows Broadcom’s EPS projections over the next five fiscal years. As seen, the EPS is projected to nearly quadruple during this period, starting from an estimate of $6.74 for the full fiscal year 2025 to $22.17 by FY 2029. The market expected consistent YOY growth in the range of about 26% to as high as 40% in the next few years. This massive growth potential helps to justify high valuation ratios. To wit, the forward FY1 P/E ratio for 2025 stands at more than 56x as seen.

With the anticipated annual growth rate between 26% and 40%, the implied PEG (P/E growth) ratio is around ~1.4x to ~2.0x. It is a bit on the elevated side compared to the 1x ideal threshold most GARP (growth at reasonable growth) investors seek, but it is nothing outlandish in my view given the quality of its business model.

To close, this AVGO follow-up was triggered by its upcoming ER for Q4. A closer examination of its recent financial statements and also its Q4 EPS revisions shows a sizable and persisting chasm between its GAAP and normalized earnings. Based on my analyses, I believe the chasm is mostly due to its treatment of acquisition/integration costs and also stock-based compensation. I feel these treatments are debatable in the case of the AVGO and would pay special attention to these items in the FQ4 ER.

data.title: Alphabet: Shares Rise Sharply For AI-Charged Company, But Still A Long-Term Play
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

AI has been the main theme soaking up all the limelight in 2025, and that has been generating a lot of buzz around certain companies. Primarily in the technology space, and one of those companies is Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), seeing a major increase in its Morningstar fair value estimate with our latest update. This is a more direct relation to the AI technology focus, but we also saw lifts in Caterpillar (CAT) and DTE Energy (DTE), which also noted AI being a key role for lifting price targets.

While Google's search dominance is continually under question, it continually outperforms expectations despite the calls for ChatGPT and other AI to disrupt it significantly. To be fair, it could still happen in the future; we just haven't been seeing any evidence of it yet. In fact, its latest earnings report showed that Google search "and other" was up 14.5%, helping the company hit the $100 billion revenue mark in a single quarter for the first time.

GOOGL shares have been rocketing higher this year and, in particular, the last few months. However, they are not trading over the lofty fair value estimated price, either, so taking a bit of a deeper look at this name to see if it is still a bargain. For a bit of a spoiler, it doesn't appear overly expensive for a longer-term investor who can sit pat for years instead of trying to make a quick buck.

Is GOOGL Still Cheap?

GOOGL is clearly an AI-focused company in the technology space, with its most dominant segment as search, but all the competition is expected when it comes to AI assistants. Unfortunately, or fortunately perhaps for GOOG, AI searches can be less reliable and provide inaccurate information. I know when I'm using any of them, I often find errors, but it is admittedly often on more niche or complex topics for investing.

With that said, GOOGL has been focused on their Gemini AI product and implementing that into as many applications as they can in order to gain market share in the space. ChatGPT is the leader, followed by Microsoft (MSFT) Copilot, but then we have Google's Gemini, with one site noting that Gemini is 13.4% AI search market share (both Copilot and Gemini source to this same site).

So Search isn't dying as some would expect, as that segment rose 14.5% year-over-year, and Alphabet is competing in the AI space, not throwing the towel in and giving up. With those strong results that drove GOOGL to top the $100 billion revenue per quarter mark, it sent shares higher, but being on the back of such solid results, it seemed warranted.
